title: The Same God in Both Testaments
executive_summary: This is the article I've been most afraid to write. Not because the answer is unclear — the answer has been sitting in plain view for sixteen centuries. I've been afraid because the question is one of the most painful in Christianity, and most of the answer... For most of Christian history, that claim has been an act of faith more than an act of reason. The packet separates publishable language from unresolved math, axiom, and contradiction obligations.
plain_english: This is the article I've been most afraid to write. Not because the answer is unclear — the answer has been sitting in plain view for sixteen centuries. I've been afraid because the question is one of the most painful in Christianity, and most of the answers people give to it have made the pain w... It is late afternoon in the southern Levant. The air smells like woodsmoke and sun-baked mud and, faintly, the metallic tang of blood from the morning's animal slaughter. You are standing on a footpath outside a walled village. There is no police station. There are no courts. There is no hospital... You are not from here. You came from the future. You are wearing the Western armchair you carried with you, and from inside that armchair you have always wondered why the God of the Old Testament was so harsh. You are about to find out that you were asking the wrong question. In simpler terms, the article is trying to establish: For most of Christian history, that claim has been an act of faith more than an act of reason. The math layer has been extracted separately so equations can be translated and checked without blurring them into the prose.

contradictions.text: You count six houses on this side of the path, and you should be seeing twenty or thirty children running between them, but you are seeing eight.
She is carrying a fifth one now and praying to a god whose name you do not know yet that this one will live long enough to be named.
You do not know it yet, but two-thirds of everyone in this village is carrying a parasitic infection from that water — a tiny worm that lives in their liver and slowly destroys it.
The treatment will sometimes work, by accident — quarantine reduces transmission whether you understand transmission or not — but nobody in this village is doing science.
You will not be able to articulate exactly when it changed, but it changed.
But it has a fatal problem, and the problem isn't a Bible verse — it's the moral standard the critic is using.
But the critic is treating it as binding when she uses it to judge God.
But pushed to the limit, this answer becomes a blank check.
But the answer leaves a question hanging that it can't address: why so slow?
They're all trying to resolve the same underlying tension.

The tension is this: **How can the same Person be perfectly just AND perfectly merciful AND preserve human freedom — at the same time, in the same world, without any of the three collapsing into the other two?** That's the question.
The sovereigntist relaxes the constraint that God's actions be intelligible to human moral reasoning — if it's a black box, the contradiction is unspeakable.

math_items.expression: > [!quote] _"For I the Lord do not change."_ — Malachi 3:6
> [!quote] _"Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever."_ — Hebrews 13:8
The deep research source document with full citation chains lives at `_STAGING\BRONZE_AGE_RESEARCH_SOURCE.md`.
_What we got right, what we're less sure about, and where we got carried away._
_The article above is what we believe. This audit is what we know we haven't proven yet. Both matter._

claims.text: For most of Christian history, that claim has been an act of faith more than an act of reason.
The actions in the Old Testament are immoral by any standard you care to apply, therefore the God who commanded them is either evil, flawed, or fictional.
We do not claim to have captured God in equations.
We claim that when we look at His creation honestly — with the tools of physics and the revelation of Scripture — the same structure appears in both.

This is the hardest passage in the framework and it deserves the longest sit. **The Canaanite child sacrifice claim is real but its scope is debated.** The Phoenician *tophets* and the cremation patterns in infant remains are well-attested at multiple sites including Carthage, Motya, and Tharros.

A reader who wants to dig in should know this is contested ground at the margins, even though the core archaeological finding is solid. **"Love your neighbor" as graduation, not orientation, is a strong claim about hermeneutics.** I think it's right.
The claim is that the meaning of *love your neighbor* is fully present in Jesus' teaching only because the curriculum that defines its terms has finished running by the time He delivers it.
That's a claim about *how* the teaching becomes intelligible, not a claim about its *importance*.

open_obligations: Review suspected contradictions/tensions and mark as resolved, live tension, or true contradiction.
Translate each extracted equation into plain English and verify the isomorphism claim.
Attach evidence, axiom links, and falsification criteria to the strongest claims.
